speaker: interviewer
text: It's tape four. I'm sorry. We're interviewing Shlomo Berger in Los Angeles, California, USA. Go ahead, Shlomo.

speaker: interviewee
text: The man asked, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I said [SPEAKING POLISH]. That-- that means, I don't understand German. The biggest mistake the Jewish people that pretended to be Christian made, when they-- when they talked German. The minute they talked German, the German knew, because Poles hardly knew any German.

speaker: interviewer
text: He ask again in-- in-- in-- in German, what did you come here for? I don't understand German. I'm showing him the documents. And the Ukrainian policeman is translating to me. He is asking you what did you come here for? And I said, I got an order from the arbeitzamp to come to-- to present myself to go to work to Germany. Looked straight in his eyes. Didn't give a blink.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he looks at me, and I look at him, and he finally hands me back over the documents. And he says, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. That means--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: --go. Now, if I didn't die at that time, I'll live a million years.

speaker: interviewer
text: I hope so.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I came back to town, and I was looking what's going to happen to find out, like they say, a spy. And I found out that since the German did not keep the promise to the Ukrainians and never had intentions to give them independence, that was western Ukraine, the Ukrainians probably were going to make a revolution. They had a conference over there of all high command German officers. But the Germans found out what was going on. And the whole city was on alert, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and nothing happened. I went back to my place over there, to-- gave them the news, gave them the information, and that thing was going on until the 29th of April, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: One thing I want to ask you. You never suffered of real hunger?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only time I was hungry was in prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was in prison. OK. Go ahead, I'm sorry. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, because now-- they asked me before, where did you get the food? What did you get to eat? Now, the farmers used to give us wheat. These were their parents. These were their family. [INTERPOSING VOICES] These were their relatives.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to come in at night into the villages. We used to get to eat. Sometimes we even slept in the villages. So I did not suffer any hunger.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: April, 1944-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we saw already the tanks-- the Russian tanks-- coming close to town, close to the place where we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of it again?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- for heaven's sake.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're doing very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I know it was-- it was about-- about 20 kilometers east of Stanislawow. Stanislawow--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --was a big town. It's called now-- they changed the name of it-- [NON-ENGLISH], or something like that. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --at that time it was Stanislawow. And so we tried to be heroes. And we coming into town, and we want to take the town before the Russians will walk in. And there was a-- a Hungarian regiment was stationed over there. They were-- they were fighting alongside the Germans. They caught us, [INTERPOSING VOICES] arrested us, put us in prison. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And a German guard was standing over-- two German guards were standing over. We were all supposed to get executed. In the middle of the night, Hungarians came. They killed the two Germans, and they let us out. They knew what was going to happen to them because the Russians were 10 kilometers from there. And in the morning, the Russian tanks came in. The whole Hungarian regiment they threw away their arms. And they walked into the-- to the Soviet prison. Now, we are free people-- no more-- no more German occupation. Unfortunately, or fortunately, whatever-- whichever you want to say, the front stopped right there. The Germans were in Stanislawow. And we were 15-- 15 or 10 kilometers outside. All of a sudden the Germans made a counteroffensive, and the Russian Soviet army started to withdraw. I'm not waiting for [? cover. ?] I'm-- I'm withdrawing together with them. And during the time of the withdrawal, the German Stuka planes and Messerschmidts, they used to dive over us, and they used to throw bombs and firing the machine guns. And I remember I was laying in a field, bare. It was April. Nothing was growing yet. And I dug my head in into the ground, and I look, and the bullets are flying here, and the bullets flying in the other side. And the bombs are flying in front of me. They're flying in the back of me. Somehow I came out in one piece.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you wear a Russian uniform at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No I-- we-- I-- we were wearing civilian clothes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And finally we kept on going. We kept on withdrawing until to Bukovyna, which was part of Romania, near Czernowitz, the town. That's when we established the-- the-- the-- the front. I mean the Soviet army established itself. And the group that they were in the partisan group, quite a few withdrew together with-- with the Russians. And they put us into a [NON-ENGLISH] they used to call [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. In other words, we were doing guard duty. At the same time we were under the supervision of a NKVD officer. Today they call it KGB. We had to take political training. We learned the language. We were learning-- we were learning Russian. And the front was standing there for approximately three months. They were bringing in Russian-- I mean German prisoners, and I was doing translating from German to Russian for the kommandantur. Finally, after about three months, artillery started firing-- Russian artillery-- firing for about 48 hours without a stop. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The whole-- they scorched the earth. And when they made a move, there was not a German in front in sight of them. And the front started to move for approximately 100 kilometers, till it stopped around [INAUDIBLE] near the river [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: I go back. We go back to the town that we are coming from, the-- the partisan group. It didn't take long, and there was what do you call a [NON-ENGLISH] in-- in-- in-- in English? They-- they went ahead-- the Russian army, the Soviet army, all young people that they were on the street, they grabbed them, took him into the kommandantur. You're in the army now, including me. Well, I didn't think like anybody else, because my-- my-- I wanted to survive that war. In a bad way I wanted to survive that war after going all that through. My mind kept still working, and since I was very acquainted with that commandant that was interviewing the German prisoners, and I was doing the translating, and he took over the kommandantur in that town, he was [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. I mean, he was a wounded officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: A wounded man.

speaker: interviewee
text: A wounded officer, that he was a commandant all the time in the--in the the back land. I convinced him that he should keep me as his adjutant, and I'll be his translator for the German prisoners. I know the local population. I know Russian. I know Polish. I know German. I can get you anything you want. And especially after I gave him my gold watch that I took away from a German prisoner, he says, you're here. But in order to keep me, he had to give me a rank. So he sent me in for four weeks' political indoctrination and training. And I came back as a second lieutenant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still under the Polish name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Under the Polish name. Never changed the name. I became instead Jan Jerzowski. I became Ivan [? Marianovich ?] Jerzowski. The father-- the father was [? Marion. ?] That was my name. I never wanted to admit I had anything to do with Jews, because that division over there was a Ukrainian division-- the second Ukranski front. They were just as anti-Semitic as-- as all the other Poles. Anyway, as the front moved forward, I moved with them. I stayed with them all the time until the fronts moved to [POLISH] in Silesia. At that time, I took a leave of absence. I came back to my town, Krosno. I came to see who survived. The house we used to live was all demolished. I found approximately 25 Jews living in town from all-- from all the area, not just from the town. And there was one house over there that used to belong to a lady. Her name was Mrs. Fishbein. She later-- she later went to Israel. And I came to visit her, and all of a sudden I see there's two representatives over there from [INAUDIBLE], that they are involved in the Bricha They are involved in the Bricha because this was the last stop where Jews were leaving Poland, the survivors, toward Czechoslovakia, because they told them, they said everybody wants to leave Poland. I came in there, was introduced by that Mrs. Fishbein that knew me from before. And they asked me to be helpful to the Bricha. Since I had, since I was a Soviet officer, I had access to any place I can go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they sent me to Warsaw to see Antik Zuckerman. And Antik Zuckerman was at that time a member of the Central Jewish Committee in Warsaw. The president of the Jewish Committee name was a Mr. [? Biederman. ?] He was communist. That Antik Zuckerman asked me to go ahead, and since I am-- I am a Soviet officer, he'll be talking to me. I should find out what they know about the Bricha.

speaker: interviewee
text: I walked in to him. I introduced myself as a Jan Jerzowski, but my previous name was Berger. I am from Krosno. I'm a member of the Communist Party. I-- I had somebody, a friend in Krosno over there made me a-- a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] what they say a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, paper.

speaker: interviewee
text: --paper that I'm a member of the Communist Party. And he says, you're the right man that I need. He says, we know that Jews are leaving Poland. And there is a Zionist organization that's leading them out. We don't have any objections to the Jews leaving Poland. We want to know who the leaders are, because we don't want that to happen. We want them to stay in Poland. Anyway, I told this to Antik Zuckermann, what is happening. And I decided at that time that that's the end of me being in Poland. I took a group of these survivors from Warsaw to Lublin. I dumped them off there at the committee. Then I took a group from Lublin to Krakow, came into the Jewish committee in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I walked into the room over there, and there's a beautiful girl standing there. And I asked the girl that-- that was in charge of that committee, who is she? She says, she's a friend from my town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I fell in love with that girl when I saw her the first time. And I said to me, I'm going to marry this girl. I'm not going to let her get away. I asked her out, and she wouldn't go. So I found out where she lives. She was living with a Polish officer's wife, that he was in the Polish English army over there. And it's a Polish girl. She was also a survivor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I come there at nighttime after curfew. I knock at the door. That lady opens the door. I say is [POLISH] here? That was her name. She says, no, But she wants to close the door in front of me. So I put my foot in the door, stuck my foot in the door, and I pushed the door open. And here is that girl standing in the middle of the room. I say, you son of a gun. I say, get out of here to the-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] to the frau.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all I said to my present wife-- now, I know your friend told me that you want to leave Poland. You didn't find anybody surviving here, and you don't have any money. I'm leading out a group tomorrow. I'm going to leave Poland. If you want to come, by 10 o'clock in the morning, I want you to show up in the committee. That's all I said.

speaker: interviewer
text: After I left, that woman gave her hell. She says, I treat you so good, like my own child. You bringing me the KGB here? I had a special uniform with a black cap and with a big gun with all that stuff over there. She got very scared. So my-- this girl decided she's not going to be there anymore. And I came at 10 o'clock in the morning to the committee. Here she was with her bag-- with her valise.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I took a group. We went to the railroad station. We're going to go to Krosno. The train is full of people, all these cattle trains. We couldn't even get in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I hustled up a few Russian soldiers. We opened the door from one of these cattle trains. We threw everybody out. Everybody out. We got into the cattle car, we closed the door, and we had it all for ourselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Krosno. It was the last part of April, 1945. At that time, I decided, this is it. I overstayed my leave. I'm probably AWOL, what they say a deserter. I don't want to be in the Russian army. I don't want to be in Poland. There's nothing for me to be here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had this group from the Bricha. They made me up new papers, changed my name from Jerzowski to Harare, Schlomo Harare-- that means Berger. I became a Greek. And I'm going home back to Greece.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took a group with me to the train. We boarded the train to go for Czechoslovakia. I'm still in uniform. And I prepared for myself a striped uniform from concentration camp. This was what everybody was wearing. And while we were traveling to Czechoslovakia, in the middle, I took off my uniform. I tore up all the military papers, all the military pictures that I had. I threw it out, and I put on a striped uniform. And I became Shlomo Harare, a surviving Greek. We came into Czechoslovakia. People were waiting at the train-- the train stations. And they were bringing in food, and they were bringing in clothes. And they came to greet all the survivors. At that time, we undressed of our stripes, and we put on regular clothes. They brought us clothes. And we kept on going until we came to a Hungarian town that's called Debrecen. In Debrecen we stayed approximately a week, 10 days. I don't remember exactly. On the 9th of May 1945, we hear cannons firing. Music is playing. Everybody is hollering and demonstrating. We go out into the street to find out what's happening in here. Germany capitulated. The war ended. Well, now we are really free.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from over there, we were left already by the Bricha. We came to a Romanian town, which was called Cluj. And in Cluj, they were-- they were-- they-- they were accumulating over there refugees to keep on going further, because everybody wanted to go to Palestine. And the young lady was still with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she is going with me. So while we are in Cluj, we go into a park. And-- and we-- we sit, and we talk. And finally I ask her to marry me. And she said, no. And I say, yes. And she said, no. I talk so long until she said, yes. I wouldn't give up. We came back to the villa. We had a little rabbi over there, a so-called rabbi. And we said, hurry up, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And we got married. That's-- that was our marriage. And we became a married couple. And we keep on going. We came to Bucharest. We came to Bucharest, and they had a big building over there with all these survivors. In the same building was-- what was name, that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the leader of the-- of the-- of the partisans, the Jewish partisans in Vilna there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh-- Bielski.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bielski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bielski.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bielski was there at the same time in the same place. Anyway, we were supposed to go. From the Black Sea was supposed to be ships to go to Palestine. But there were no ships-- nothing. So now we've got to keep on going. Got to go to Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they gave up-- made up for us International Red Cross passports, stateless people, all counterfeit. And all of a sudden, Shlomo Harare became an Italian.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I became an Italian. We were going. We came into Yugoslavia. We came into Belgrade. We stayed in Belgrade a little bit. And then from Belgrade, we took the train. We came into Italy. In Italy, in [NON-ENGLISH] was a transit camp. It was run by the Jewish Brigade already from Palestine. They registered us. They gave us food. And they said, well, all the ships to Palestine are in the southern Italy. After a few days, they took us to the train, and they shipped us to Bari, which was in southern Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was a displaced camp, wasn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from Bari, there was-- the train didn't go anymore. They had trucks there. And they took us to a displaced person that's called Santa Maria di Bagni.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that in Sicily?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was Calabria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Calabira.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's at the heel of the-- of the map. Now, we were one of the very few that-- that came in first over there. We established a kibbutz. And every night they used to come over there. The kibbutz was Lachovich. That means freedom. And every night they used to come from the-- from the hagenah. And they used to grab out groups, took them to the little ships. We were right on the sea. They took them to larger little ships, and they used to take them to-- to supposedly for the illegal immigration.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aliyah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Aliyah. I was not called, because when we came over there, they registered party affiliations. And me knowing what I did and all that stuff, I wasn't going to lie. I said I was-- I used to be in Betar. Anyway, the Betar did not work over there and the hagenah did not take any Betarim.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- we stayed there for a while until-- and finally, my wife got pregnant. And when the time came that they wanted to take me already, I wouldn't go. And I knew already-- I found out in the meantime-- that I have three sisters in Los Angeles. I remembered one of my sisters' address. I found out through the Red Cross that my brother-- younger brother-- Mike survived from Auschwitz and the other camps, that he is also in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, we decided we were going to go to America. So we are staying in Italy now. It lasted three years. I got a job. First, I was the camp secretary, elected camp secretary. And then I-- then I became an art instructor. I was teaching pattern making and designing for about two years in three camps. And I was getting paid. I was getting paid by the American Joint. And eventually, for three years, we staying there. And 1947, the American consulate opened the registration to go to America. So I went to Naples, got into the-- to the American consulate. I got a registration number-- very low-- 182nd person from the Polish quarter-- 182, 183, 184, because we already had a child. And I thought, well, since it's such a low number, in the hundreds I probably going to go soon to America. But they let in 50 people born in Poland, and they closed the immigration for the Polish quarters. So we're staying there. It's already 1948. And I don't want to be in camps anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was acquainted over there with a welfare officer, a Jewish girl from London. She says, Berger, I can help you if you want to. If you're willing to go to London, I can get you to London. So I says I'll go anyplace, just away from here. She says, I'm going to write to my brother-in-law. He is a very wealthy person.

speaker: interviewee
text: Less than three weeks, I got papers from England to come as a domestic resident couple. That means I'm supposed to be the butler, and my wife supposed to be the maid. And they-- we went to the-- to the English consulate. They gave us a six-months visa to go to England. We came there. He didn't need me, but he found me a job right away. I went to work in a shop, was making nice money. We got an apartment-- my wife and my child. We went every day to the nursery. And we stayed there for two years, and we had a pretty nice life. After about six months that we came to England, usually I'm supposed to get extended my visa all the time-- the extension, like all my friends over there had to do every six months. One day, a policeman comes into my house and says to my wife, we would like your husband to come to the police station. She says, he's at work. He says, OK, Saturday is OK. You'll have him bring all the documents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Saturday I came into the police station. And I-- and he says to me, you know you're supposed to be a domestic resident couple. How come you don't live in the place over there where you-- where you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, well, these people have a small house. They don't have enough room for a family, but they don't mind if I come to work every day. He says, I know you're going to work every day. Because he-- they-- they probably watched me. The bus station was in the front of the house. Every day in the morning, I used to go up with a brown lunch bag, going to work six days a week. He asked me some more questions. He says, please wait. Came into his office. Came back 15 minutes later, and he gave me a permanent residence of the United Kingdom. In other words, I don't have to register any more. I can do anything I please. This was unheard of. After six months, I got a permanent residency. And after five years I could have become a citizen, but it took about two years. I was called to the English consulate. The number that I registered in Italy came up from immigration. They called me in. They gave me a visa to come to America. And we left May, 1950. We came into New York had a big family in New York, stayed a week. Then we went to Chicago for a week, and we came into Los Angeles. I stayed in Los Angeles one day. The next day I had a job, and I worked for 30 years without a stop. We had another child in Los Angeles. We established ourself very nicely. This country was very good to me. Within a year, I had a car. Within two years, I bought a house. I educated my children. My son is-- is-- is a medical doctor. My daughter is a very educated person. She is a Psychology major. And we have beautiful grandchildren. We have a beautiful life-- something that I could never have in Poland even if I stayed there for 2,000 years. And that's about the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I hope you live to be 100 and have more stories to tell.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there enough left? Go ahead, Shlomo. What was your wife-- what is your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in my story here, I never mentioned the names of my wife, how she got through the war. I never mention-- that has nothing-- I never mentioned my children. [INTERPOSING VOICES] My wife's name originally was Gusta [? Friedmann ?]. Now, when we came to America my sisters didn't like Gusta, so they named her Gertrude, and she hates that name all the time. She would rather change her name back Gusta. My children's name, my son's name is Jack, after my father. He was born in Italy. He's now 49 years old. He's a physician in Los Angeles. [INTERPOSING VOICES] My daughter is Marlene. Each one have two children. My son has a daughter named Tara and a-- and a boy, Alex. He's going to the University of California. My daughter has two sons, Daniel and Jesse. They are all happy families. They all have their homes. And we have a very close family together here.

speaker: interviewer
text: They all live in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewee
text: They all live in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's good. Go ahead, tell me. One thing I wanted to make sure is-- really, I've interviewed a lot of people and nobody's ever seen Hitler. You are the only one that saw Hitler.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess it was my luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Luck? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Or maybe it wasn't. Now, if I would at that time, probably, I would wanted to die, and if I would-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] have a bomb--

speaker: interviewer
text: You would have thrown it.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and if I would throw it in his open car, maybe it will save ourselves all these Jews, all the 3 million Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know, but you didn't have a bomb.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well. I didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But if you had, I'm sure you would have thrown it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because the way I've been interviewing you, I know that you would have thrown it if you had a bomb with you.
